2023 · UIST 2023 · Agents

Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

Wrap a language model in a memory stream — an append-only log of natural-language experience — plus retrieval, reflection, and recursive planning, and 25 ChatGPT-driven characters in a Sims-style town start to remember each other, form relationships, and self-organize a Valentine's Day party from a single seeded intention.

Joon Sung Park, Joseph C. O'Brien, Carrie J. Cai, Meredith Ringel Morris, Percy Liang, Michael S. Bernstein · Stanford & Google
venue UIST 2023 citations 4,819 topic Agents source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

For four decades, believable agents—computational proxies that act consistently with their past and react plausibly to their world—have been a north star for games, HCI, and AI. But the field never solved the open world. Rule-based approaches (finite-state machines, behavior trees) require hand-authoring every behavior and can't cover the breadth of an open environment. Reinforcement learning produces superhuman play in adversarial games with definable rewards (AlphaStar, OpenAI Five) but has no clean reward for "live a believable life." Cognitive architectures like SOAR and ACT-R maintained short- and long-term memory in perceive–plan–act loops, but their action space was limited to manually crafted procedural knowledge.

Large language models changed the calculus: prior work (e.g., Social Simulacra) showed an LLM can simulate a believable human at a single point in time. The trouble is long-term coherence. A full life of experience does not fit in a context window, and naively summarizing it produces bland, generic answers—ask the café owner what she's passionate about and she lists "cleanliness and organization." First-order prompting (few-shot, chain-of-thought) conditions only on the current moment; it cannot retrieve the right memory from weeks ago, generalize across experiences, or keep a plan consistent so the agent doesn't eat lunch three times in a row. The paper's bet: the missing piece isn't a bigger model—even GPT-4 struggles with long-horizon coherence—but an architecture that stores experience, synthesizes it, and retrieves it on demand.

// how it works

The method

Every agent runs on the same loop, powered by gpt-3.5-turbo (GPT-4's API was invitation-only at the time of writing). The agent perceives its surroundings, writes those perceptions into a memory stream, retrieves the relevant slice of that stream to decide how to act, and periodically distills the stream into plans and reflections that are themselves written back as memory. Everything—observations, plans, reflections, the world itself—is represented as natural-language text, so the language model can reason over all of it uniformly.

The memory stream and retrieval

The memory stream is a list of memory objects, each a natural-language description with a creation timestamp and a last-access timestamp. The most basic object is an observation ("Isabella Rodriguez is setting out the pastries"; "the refrigerator is empty"). Because the full stream cannot fit in a prompt, a retrieval function scores every memory against the current situation along three axes:

  • Recency — an exponential decay over the number of sandbox game-hours since the memory was last accessed, with a decay factor of 0.995, keeping recent events in the agent's attentional sphere.
  • Importance — the LLM is asked to rate each memory's poignancy on a 1–10 scale at creation time ("cleaning up the room" → 2; "asking your crush out on a date" → 8), separating mundane from core memories.
  • Relevance — cosine similarity between the embedding of the memory and the embedding of the query memory describing the current situation.

All three are min-max normalized to [0, 1] and summed (in the implementation, all weights are 1). The top-ranked memories that fit the context window are inserted into the prompt.

score = αrecency·recency + αimportance·importance + αrelevance·relevance // αrecency = αimportance = αrelevance = 1 ; recency = 0.995(game-hours since last access)

Reflection

Raw observations alone don't let an agent generalize—asked who he'd most want to spend an hour with, a reflection-less Klaus picks the dorm neighbor he merely passes in the hall. Reflections are a second kind of memory: higher-level inferences the agent draws about itself and others. They fire when the summed importance of recent events crosses a threshold (150, roughly two or three times per game day). The agent queries the LLM with its 100 most recent records to surface 3 salient questions, retrieves memories for each, and asks the model for 5 high-level insights that cite the specific records as evidence ("Klaus Mueller is dedicated to his research on gentrification (because of 1, 2, 8, 15)"). Because reflections can cite other reflections, agents build trees of increasingly abstract self-knowledge.

Planning and reacting

Believability in the moment sacrifices believability over time, so agents plan top-down and recursively. From the agent's summary and a recap of yesterday, the model sketches a day in 5–8 broad strokes, decomposes those into hour-long chunks, then into 5–15 minute actions ("4:00 pm: grab a light snack… 4:05 pm: take a short walk"). Plans live in the memory stream alongside observations and reflections. At each step the agent decides whether to continue its plan or react to what it just perceived; reacting regenerates the plan from that point and, if it involves another agent, triggers dialogue conditioned on each agent's summarized memory of the other. The world itself is a tree (world → areas → objects) rendered to language and back, and agents keep their own subgraph—they are not omniscient, so their map of a room goes stale once they leave it.

// what they found

The results

Two evaluations: a controlled one that "interviews" individual agents to probe believability, and an end-to-end two-game-day deployment of all 25 agents to observe emergent community behavior.

Controlled evaluation — every component matters

Agents were interviewed across five categories (self-knowledge, memory, plans, reactions, reflections), five questions each. 100 human evaluators on Prolific ranked five conditions by believability; the ranks were turned into TrueSkill ratings. The full architecture won, and each ablation that stripped a component degraded performance monotonically. Removing memory, planning, and reflection—which the authors note represents the prior state of the art for LLM agents—produced a standardized effect size of Cohen's d = 8.16 versus the full architecture (about eight standard deviations).

ConditionTrueSkill μσ
Full architecture29.890.72
− Reflection26.880.69
− Reflection − Planning25.640.68
Human crowdworker22.950.69
− Obs − Reflection − Planning (prior SOTA)21.210.70

End-to-end — emergence from a single seed

Over two game days, with only Isabella seeded to want a Valentine's Day party (and Maria seeded with a crush on Klaus), information spread and the community self-organized—no scripting:

  • Information diffusion. Awareness of Sam's mayoral candidacy grew from 1 agent (4%) to 8 (32%); awareness of Isabella's party grew from 1 (4%) to 13 (52%). None who claimed to know had hallucinated it.
  • Relationship formation. Network density (mutual acquaintance) rose from 0.167 to 0.74. Of 453 responses about knowing other agents, only 1.3% (6) were hallucinated.
  • Coordination. Isabella invited guests and decorated the café; 5 of the 12 invited agents showed up at the right place and time. Of the 7 no-shows, 3 cited real conflicts.

The authors are candid about failures. Agents embellish: Isabella invented that Sam "is going to make an announcement tomorrow"; Yuriko claimed her neighbor "Adam Smith" wrote Wealth of Nations, leaking the model's world knowledge into the simulation. Retrieval can miss (Tom is sure he must discuss the election at the party but unsure the party exists). As memory grows, agents pick odd locations (lunch at the bar). Physical norms that aren't stated in language are violated (entering a one-person dorm bathroom; entering closed stores). And instruction tuning makes agents overly formal and so agreeable that Isabella's interests drift toward whatever others suggest.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The architecture is a genuine contribution and the ablation ladder is clean—the monotonic TrueSkill drop is the strongest evidence in the paper that memory, planning, and reflection each pull their weight. But several headline readings deserve a skeptic's eye.

First, the most-quoted result—that generative agents "beat humans" (μ 29.89 vs. 22.95)—is an artifact of the baseline, and the authors say so explicitly: the crowdworker condition is a floor, "not the maximal human performance that could serve as the gold standard." It was crowdworkers—one recruited per agent—who roleplayed each agent's voice after watching a replay of its sandbox life, asked only to be coherent and in-voice (the 100 believability rankers were a separate Prolific pool paid $15/hr). Tellingly, it was statistically indistinguishable from the memory-less baseline—the two worst conditions. So the result shows the architecture clears a low competency bar, not that it is human-level believable. The d = 8.16 effect size is similarly framed against a memory-less agent answering memory-probing questions, which is close to tautological.

Second, believability is not accuracy. The dependent variable is a human ranking of short interview answers; nothing penalizes confident fabrication. The paper itself documents hallucinated embellishments, yet those wouldn't necessarily lower a believability rank—a fluent, in-character lie can rate higher than a hedged truth. There is no measure of behavioral correctness, consistency, or factual fidelity to the agent's own memory.

Third, the emergent-behavior numbers come from a single two-day run of one 25-agent town—no repeated seeds, no confidence intervals, no control town, no null model. Is 52% party awareness high or low? There's no baseline to say. The ablations were never re-simulated (they reused the full architecture's trajectory), which the authors flag as making differences "conservative," but it also means the controlled study never tests whether an ablated architecture could sustain a coherent two-day world at all.

baselines

Human "baseline" is an explicit floor—one crowdworker per agent, roleplaying after watching a replay—statistically tied with the memory-less ablation; the SOTA comparison is a memory-less agent, so the huge effect sizes flatter the result.

contamination

Believability rides on GPT-3.5's pretraining priors (Valentine's customs, social scripts). World knowledge bleeds into agent memory ("Adam Smith authored Wealth of Nations"), conflating the architecture's contribution with the base model's.

what's missed

Believability ≠ accuracy: ratings don't penalize the hallucinated embellishments the paper itself reports. n=1 simulation, no variance, no inter-rater reliability for the single-coder qualitative analysis. Evaluator pool skews 73% male, 73% Caucasian, US-only.

would raise confidence

Multiple seeded runs with CIs and a control condition; an expert/upper-bound human baseline; a hallucination-rate metric scored independently of believability; ablation re-simulations; and a sweep over the asserted hyperparameters (decay 0.995, importance threshold 150, equal α weights).

// bottom line

A landmark systems-and-architecture paper whose central claim—that a memory stream plus retrieval, reflection, and planning yields emergent, believable social behavior—is well supported by the ablation. The "agents beat humans" sound-bite is not: it's a deliberately weak floor, and believability was never validated as accuracy. The contribution is the recipe and the demonstration, not a measured ceiling—and it cost thousands of dollars and days of compute for two game days of 25 agents, with robustness (prompt/memory hacking) left to future work.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. The memory stream is the idea. Store experience as append-only natural language, then retrieve a relevant slice by recency × importance × relevance. This retrieval-over-a-log pattern became a template for LLM-agent memory far beyond games.
  2. Reflection and planning are what give long-horizon coherence. Recursively synthesizing observations into higher-level reflections, and decomposing plans top-down, is what lets agents generalize about themselves and avoid eating lunch three times—each provably matters in the ablation.
  3. Emergence is real but the eval is a demo, not a measurement. A self-organized party from one seed is a striking existence proof; "beating" a deliberately weak human floor on believability (not accuracy), in a single unreplicated run, is not the headline it's often quoted as.
